blocks.content: Walk back. Maybe stop at The Copper Mug. Let the day settle into something he could understand.
But the city had a different idea.

His feet carried him slower than usual, the evening pulling long shadows across the sidewalk. Gerald hung from his hand—that ridiculous yellow duck with the crooked bow tie—catching the last of the light like it had somewhere to be.
The streets were familiar. Same buildings. Same worn pavement. Same quiet that settled over this town after dinner, when it stopped pretending to be somewhere bigger than it was.
He slowed without meaning to near the park.
The fountain sat in its usual place, still sputtering that thin, uneven stream upward like stubbornness alone kept it going.
Yesterday, it was just a fountain.
Tonight, it was where something started.
He stood at the edge of the stone path. The bench near the archway was empty. Of course it was.
But he could almost see it again—
The way she had looked at his painting like it mattered.
Ethan exhaled through his nose and kept walking.

By the time he reached his apartment, the sky had gone purple at the edges, the last of the sun folding quietly away. He set Gerald on his desk. Sat on the edge of his bed. Stared at the ceiling for a moment that stretched longer than it should have.
His phone was in his hand.
He didn't remember picking it up.
Too small for how it felt.
The reply came faster than expected.
Not a question. A demand. Nathan didn't use question marks for things that mattered.
Park. She walked up to me while I was painting.
A stranger walked up to you.
And you didn't immediately pack up and leave.
Because that was exactly what he usually did.
We talked. Went to the arcade. She works there.
Last year you said they were sensory punishment dressed up with prizes.
Ethan set the phone on the bed.
How was he supposed to explain it?

The way she looked at the world like it was offering her something she couldn't afford to miss. The way she talked—like every word was chosen but also like she couldn't help herself. The way his breathing had changed in her presence without him deciding to let it.
I don't know how to say it.
You don't have to say it.
That you actually let her in.
Which means you didn't have time to decide not to.
He was never wrong about this.
